name: Old Persian Cuneiform
description: A semi-alphabetic cuneiform script developed specifically for Old Persian, used in Achaemenid royal inscriptions. Simpler than Mesopotamian cuneiform with only 36 phonetic characters plus 8 logograms.
notableTexts: Behistun Inscription, inscriptions at Persepolis and Naqsh-e Rostam

name: Avestan Script
description: A writing system developed specifically for recording the Avesta, Zoroastrianism's sacred texts. Features 37 consonants and 16 vowels, allowing very precise phonetic representation of Middle Iranian phonology.

name: Pahlavi Scripts
description: A family of Iranian writing systems derived from Aramaic, including Inscriptional Pahlavi, Book Pahlavi, and Psalter Pahlavi. Used for Middle Persian (Pahlavi) with extensive use of Aramaic heterograms (ideograms).
notableTexts: Dēnkard, Bundahishn, inscriptions at Naqsh-e Rostam

name: Sogdian Script
description: An Aramaic-derived script used for the Sogdian language along the Silk Road. Known for extensive commercial, Buddhist, Christian, and Manichaean texts. Parent script of Old Uyghur and Mongolian scripts.
notableTexts: Ancient Letters, Buddhist texts from Dunhuang, Manichaean manuscripts

name: Arabic Script (Nastaliq)
description: The dominant writing system for modern Iranian languages. Nastaliq is a calligraphic style particularly favored for Persian, Dari, and Urdu. Features modified Arabic with 4 additional letters (پ چ ژ گ) for Persian phonemes.

name: Cyrillic Script
description: Adopted for Tajik (1940), Ossetic (1937), and Yagnobi under Soviet influence. Replaced Perso-Arabic and Latin scripts in Central Asia. Includes modified letters for Iranian phonemes.

name: Latin Script
description: Used for Kurdish (Kurmanji) in Turkey and Syria, adopted in 1930s. Also used for Zaza and some online Persian (Fingilish/Pinglish). Features diacritics for Iranian phonemes (ê, î, ş, ç).

name: Manichaean Script
description: A distinctive script developed for Manichaeism, used for Middle Persian, Parthian, Sogdian, and Bactrian texts. Characterized by clear, upright letters distinct from other Aramaic-derived scripts.

name: Georgian Script
description: Historically used for some Ossetic texts before Cyrillic adoption. The Georgian script was adapted to write Ossetic in Georgia, particularly in South Ossetia.

name: Unwritten Languages
description: Several endangered Iranian languages remain primarily oral, including Munji, Sanglechi-Ishkashimi, Parachi, and Ormuri. These languages face significant preservation challenges.
